factors.summary: Talent retention is not explicitly addressed, but the company is investing in employee-related expenses, suggesting efforts to retain staff.

evidence.text: Our employee-related expenses increased in 2021 from 2020. This was related to: improvement in our sales and profitability generating significantly higher bonuses and commissions; higher health insurance costs as employees became comfortable again in seeking non-COVID-related health care; an increase in our profit sharing contribution; and higher full-time and part-time wages producing an increase in base pay.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth has been mixed, with strong performance in 2022 and 2021 driven by unit sales and pricing, but faced challenges such as supply chain and labor issues, and showed signs of deceleration in annual sales trends.

evidence.text: Organic revenue growth was strong, with net sales growing by 16.1% in 2022, driven by unit growth and pricing.
Higher unit sales in 2021 were a result of strong economic activity which increased demand for our products to our traditional manufacturing and construction customers.

Our sales growth is dependent primarily on our ability to attract new customers and increase our activity with existing customers within North America and abroad. However, failure to achieve any of our goals regarding our Digital Footprint, Onsites, national accounts, international capabilities, analytics, or other growth drivers could negatively impact our long-term sales and profit growth.
The 2021 DSR changes show a mix of positive and negative growth rates, indicating a deceleration compared to the strong recovery from 2020.

factors.summary: The end market momentum is mixed. While the manufacturing and construction sectors are showing strong growth, particularly in the fastener product line and industrial production, there are challenges in non-residential construction and safety products due to supply chain issues and PPE-related market declines.

evidence.text: The strength of the manufacturing economy in 2022 resulted in relatively faster growth in our more economically cyclical fastener product line over our non-fastener product lines, resulting in an increase in our gross profit percentage from product mix.

Growth was led by our manufacturing customers, with particular strength in markets involved with commodity and capital goods production. Our non-residential construction customers grew on an annual basis, but turned slightly negative in the fourth quarter.

Our non-residential construction and reseller business is heavily influenced by manufacturing, oil and gas, and infrastructure spending. In 2021, improving economic business conditions, high prices for commodities such as metals and energy, and tightening facilities utilization produced improving growth rates throughout the year.

While certain products and markets within our business continued to face difficult PPE comparisons, they were not as severe as what had been experienced in the first half of 2021, which allowed our safety products to post daily growth of 0.3% in the second half of 2021.

factors.summary: The company's revenue mix has shown conflicting trends across different product lines. While the shift towards higher-margin segments like fasteners and safety products has positively impacted gross margins, there is also a notable trend towards lower-margin segments, which has negatively affected gross profit percentages. This dual effect suggests a complex revenue mix environment that could present both opportunities and challenges for future profitability.

evidence.text: Changes in customer or product mix, downward pressure on sales prices, and changes in volume or timing of orders have caused and could continue to cause our gross profit percentage to fluctuate or decline in the future. Non-fastener products typically have a lower gross profit percentage than fasteners, and national accounts and Onsite customers typically have a lower gross profit percentage than smaller customers.

The shifts in product mix in 2022 compared to 2021 largely reflect the reversal of pandemic-related activity combined with the relative growth of our more cyclical fastener line as growth in manufacturing and construction end markets accelerated as the post-pandemic North American economy recovered.

The gross profit percentage for 2021 increased by 70 basis points based on higher product margins, primarily for safety products and overhead/organizational leverage related to higher volumes. [...] This caused our mix of lower margin COVID-related products to decline to approximately 31% of total safety product sales, improving our overall safety product margin.

factors.summary: The company demonstrated strong pricing power by successfully raising prices, which contributed significantly to net sales growth and helped mitigate the impact of inflation and supply chain constraints on gross profit. However, there are signs of downward pressure on sales prices due to deflation, cost pressure from customers, or increased competition, which could negatively impact gross profit in the future.

evidence.text: Downward pressure on sales prices due to deflation, pressure from customers to reduce costs, or increased competition could cause our gross profit percentage to decline.

Incremental pricing from actions taken at the end of 2021 and the start of 2022 further contributed to our growth, though over the course of the year we saw the inflationary pressures and supply chain constraints that catalyzed our pricing actions largely dissipate.

Price contributed 200 to 230 basis points to our net sales growth in 2021. We instituted a number of pricing events during 2021 as a means of mitigating rising product and transportation costs. As these events fell more heavily into the second half of the year, price contributed an increasing amount through the period, with price in the fourth quarter of 2021 contributing 440 to 470 basis points to net sales growth.

The gross profit percentage for 2021 increased by 70 basis points based on higher product margins, primarily for safety products and overhead/organizational leverage related to higher volumes. [...] The net impact of price/cost was also neutral for 2021, as we were able to lift prices in response to higher costs for products and transportation services.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: The company successfully raised prices, contributing to net sales growth and mitigating inflation and supply chain impacts.

factors.summary: The company's gross profit margins have experienced mixed performance, with some improvement in 2021 due to higher product margins and organizational leverage, but also faced pressures such as a shift in customer mix, higher input costs, and labor expenses, which could lead to further declines in the foreseeable future.

evidence.text: Customer and product mix have contributed to the decline of our gross profit percentage over time and will likely continue to reduce our gross profit percentage into the foreseeable future.

Our gross profit, as a percentage of net sales, was 46.1% in 2022 and 46.2% in 2021, a decrease of 10 basis points. This decrease was primarily related to three factors. First, in 2022 we experienced relatively higher growth from our large and Onsite customers, which tend to have a lower gross margin percentage than the business as a whole.
Higher product and transportation costs did have a slightly negative effect on our gross margin percentage for the full year.
The gross profit percentage for 2021 increased by 70 basis points based on higher product margins, primarily for safety products and overhead/organizational leverage related to higher volumes.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Mixed performance with pressures from customer mix, higher input costs, and labor expenses risking future declines.

factors.summary: The company faced rising input costs, particularly in metals, energy, and transportation, during 2021 and 2022 due to resource inflation and supply chain constraints. However, they implemented strategies such as price increases and cost management initiatives to mitigate these impacts. Despite these efforts, input costs continued to pose challenges, affecting gross margins negatively.

evidence.text: We experienced resource inflation in 2021 and 2022, as a strong recovery in global demand following the COVID-19 pandemic created tightness in the market for certain raw materials and energy. This caused our company and our suppliers to increase product prices to address higher input costs.

In 2022, we began to observe easing in inflationary pressures for metals (especially steel), energy, and transportation services (especially overseas containers and shipping). However, this did not translate into a reduction in inflationary pressures on our financial results for two reasons. [...] In 2022, we increased prices, sought alternative sources for products and service, and consolidated spend for products and services as a means of mitigating inflation. However, higher product and transportation costs did have a slightly negative effect on our gross margin percentage for the full year.

In 2021, we experienced significant increases in the cost of metals (especially steel), energy, and transportation (especially overseas containers and shipping). These inflationary trends meaningfully increased the cost of many of the products we purchase. We were able to mitigate the adverse effects of higher costs on our gross profit percentage in 2021 by increasing prices, seeking alternative sources for products and services, and consolidating spend for products and services.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Faced rising input costs despite mitigation strategies, impacting gross margins.

factors.summary: Labor costs are a significant factor with mixed trends. While the company managed to increase its employee headcount to support growth and operations, there are cost pressures due to higher wages, bonuses, and health insurance costs. High turnover and competition for qualified employees pose risks to profitability, and wage inflation further complicates cost management.

evidence.text: Our ability to attract, develop, and retain qualified personnel could impact labor costs, sales at existing selling locations, and the successful execution of our growth drivers. The turnover rate in the industry is high, particularly for less tenured employees.

We increased our total FTE employee headcount by 1,520. This reflects an increase in our in-market and non-in-market selling FTE employee headcount of 1,063 to support growth in the marketplace and sales initiatives targeting customer acquisition. We had an increase in our distribution center FTE employee headcount of 231 to support increasing product throughput at our facilities and to expand our local inventory fulfillment terminals (LIFTs).

Employee-related expenses include: (1) payroll (which includes cash compensation, stock option expense, and profit sharing), (2) health care, (3) personnel development, and (4) social taxes. [...] This was partly offset by a decline in health insurance costs, as the use of medical services by employees normalized following the post-pandemic catch-up activity in 2021.

Our employee-related expenses increased in 2021 from 2020. This was related to: improvement in our sales and profitability generating significantly higher bonuses and commissions; higher health insurance costs as employees became comfortable again in seeking non-COVID-related health care; an increase in our profit sharing contribution; and higher full-time and part-time wages producing an increase in base pay.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Higher labor costs due to wage inflation, turnover, and increased headcount.

factors.summary: The company is both gaining and potentially losing market share. On one hand, it is increasing its market share through a high-touch, high-tech approach and customer engagement strategies. On the other hand, it faces risks of losing market share due to increased competition from traditional and non-traditional competitors, including brick-and-mortar and online retailers. Additionally, while the company maintained a higher mix of safety products sales in 2021 compared to pre-pandemic levels, there are signs of shifts in product mix that could impact market share dynamics.

evidence.text: The ultimate aim of this 'high-tech, high-touch' approach to gaining market share is to allow us to get closer to our customers, going so far as to be right to the point of consumption within customers' facilities.

Increased competition from brick-and-mortar retailers could cause us to lose market share, reduce our prices, or increase our spending. Similarly, the emergence of online retailers could result in easier and quicker price discovery and the adoption of aggressive pricing strategies and sales methods.

The shifts in product mix in 2021 compared to 2020 reflect the impact of the pandemic. [...] The effect was to increase our mix of sales coming from fasteners and other product lines while reducing the mix of sales coming from safety products. [...] Our product categories did not fully revert to pre-pandemic levels in 2021, as our mix of safety products in 2021 of 21.2% remained meaningfully above our mix of safety products in 2019 of 17.9%

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Gains in market share through strategies but risks from competition.

factors.summary: The supply chain faced significant disruptions in 2021 and 2022 due to inflationary pressures, shipping shortages, and labor constraints, which led to product shortages and elevated transportation costs. However, by 2022, there were signs of improvement as inflationary pressures began to ease, particularly in areas like metals, energy, and transportation services. Despite these improvements, challenges persisted, particularly in smaller non-North American markets affected by geopolitical factors and evolving COVID-19 policies.

evidence.text: In 2021 and 2022, we experienced a shortage of certain products and elevated transportation costs as a result [of shipping shortages and labor constraints].
In 2022, we began to observe easing in inflationary pressures for metals (especially steel), energy, and transportation services (especially overseas containers and shipping).
The supply chains normalized and demand improved, we purchased more products through our traditional partners increasing our supplier rebates.
The first were supply chain constraints, as the rapid recovery in demand resulted in shortages in production and shipping capacity.

factors.summary: The company faces mixed impacts from interest rate changes, with evidence showing increased net interest expense in 2022 due to higher average debt balances and market rates, while 2021 showed a slight increase but manageable levels. The credit facility's variable-rate loans tied to SOFR could lead to increased financing costs if rates rise.

evidence.text: Loans under the Credit Facility generally bear interest at a rate per annum equal to Daily Simple Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) and mature on September 28, 2027. The cost of servicing any existing balances on our Credit Facility at that time could increase due to the SOFR-based interest rate provided for under our Credit Facility.

Our net interest expense was $13.6 in 2022 compared to $9.6 in 2021. We carried higher average debt balances in 2022 relative to the prior year, and specifically higher balances of variable rate credit facility debt, as a result of high sustained working capital needs and an increase in share buybacks. We also incurred higher average interest rates during the year due to changes in interest levels in the marketplace.
evidence.subsection: Introduction / ITEM 7. MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS

Our borrowings under the Credit Facility and Master Note Agreement peaked during each quarter of 2022 as follows: [...] As of December 31, 2022, we had $225.0 outstanding under the Credit Facility and had contingent obligations from letters of credit outstanding under the Credit Facility in an aggregate face amount of $36.3. As of December 31, 2022, we had loans outstanding under the Master Note Agreement of $330.0.

sentiment.label: very_positive
sentiment.rationale: Management demonstrates strong confidence in their growth strategy with significant investments in employees, technology, and capital expenditures, along with share buybacks.

factors.summary: The company's long-term strategy emphasizes high-touch, high-tech customer engagement and expansion through growth drivers like Onsite and international markets. Investments are being made in automation, supply chain improvements, and digital capabilities to support this strategy.

evidence.text: The ultimate aim of this 'high-tech, high-touch' approach to gaining market share is to allow us to get closer to our customers, going so far as to be right to the point of consumption within customers' facilities. Marrying our presence, capabilities and technologies deepens our relationships and our understanding of our customers' day-to-day opportunities and obstacles. This, in turn, enhances our ability to provide innovative and comprehensive solutions to our customers' challenges.

We expect our net capital expenditures in 2023 to be within a range of $210.0 to $230.0. This increase from 2022 reflects: spending on upgrades to and investments in automation at certain hubs; the beginning of construction of a distribution center in Utah; investment in materials to facilitate our branch conversion projects; higher spending on information technology; and investments in fleet equipment to support our network of heavy trucks.
evidence.subsection: Lease Obligations / Net Cash Used in Investing Activities

evidence.text: Our non-in-market selling employee count has grown in recent years due to an increased focus on resources to support our growth drivers, particularly Onsite and national account growth.

factors.summary: The company faces multiple key sensitivities, including geopolitical factors such as the Russo-Ukrainian War and China's COVID-19 policies, which negatively impact non-North American markets. Additionally, the company is sensitive to commodity prices, particularly metals and energy, as well as labor costs, inflation, and supply chain disruptions, which have significant implications for its operating expenses and growth.

evidence.text: These factors more than offset challenges in our smaller non-North American markets, where the effects of the Russo-Ukrainian War and China's evolving COVID-19 policies weighed on growth.

The most significant impact of these disruptions was on our growth driver signings. [...] (1) Our non-in-market selling employee count has grown in recent years due to an increased focus on resources to support our growth drivers, particularly Onsite and national account growth.

In 2021, we experienced significant increases in the cost of metals (especially steel), energy, and transportation (especially overseas containers and shipping). These inflationary trends meaningfully increased the cost of many of the products we purchase.

Our employee-related expenses increased in 2021 from 2020. This was related to: improvement in our sales and profitability generating significantly higher bonuses and commissions; higher health insurance costs as employees became comfortable again in seeking non-COVID-related health care; an increase in our profit sharing contribution; and higher full-time and part-time wages producing an increase in base pay.

factors.summary: The company is navigating an intensifying competitive environment through differentiation in technology, customer engagement, product margins, and market presence. While new entrants and market expansion pose challenges, the company's strategies seem to be effective in maintaining its competitive edge.

evidence.text: In our view, this means that companies that grow market share are those that develop differentiated capabilities that provide the greatest value to the customer.
The gross profit percentage for 2021 increased by 70 basis points based on higher product margins, primarily for safety products and overhead/organizational leverage related to higher volumes.

The non-fastener market is larger than the fastener market, we are underpenetrated in the non-fastener market relative to the fastener market, and industrial vending lends itself to sales of non-fastener products.

factors.summary: The company experienced a mixed performance in catalysts. While there was a positive increase in Onsite and FMI device signings, approaching pre-pandemic levels, indicating a recovery trend, the supply chain normalization and economic stabilization had a positive impact on gross profit. However, the signings did not meet the set goals, highlighting some challenges in achieving targets despite improvements.

evidence.text: While we did not reach the signings goals we had set out at the start of the year, we saw a meaningful increase in signings in 2022 over the prior year, and a return to near pre-pandemic levels. We signed 356 Onsites in 2022, below our goal of 375 to 400 units but above the prior year (274 signings). Similarly, we signed 20,735 FMI MEUs, below our goal of 23,000 to 25,000 MEUs but above the prior year (19,311 MEUs).

In 2021, our gross profit percentage increased when compared to the prior year. This was largely due to three factors. (1) We were able to leverage overhead/organizational expenses, absorbing certain fixed and period costs related to cyclical strength in our traditional manufacturing and construction markets. [...] The supply chains normalized and demand improved, we purchased more products through our traditional partners increasing our supplier rebates.

factors.summary: The company exhibits strong competitive advantages, including technology, scale, customer relationships, higher product margins in safety products, and a strong market presence in industrial vending. These elements collectively enhance its ability to provide innovative solutions and gain market share.

evidence.text: The ultimate aim of this 'high-tech, high-touch' approach to gaining market share is to allow us to get closer to our customers, going so far as to be right to the point of consumption within customers' facilities. Marrying our presence, capabilities and technologies deepens our relationships and our understanding of our customers' day-to-day opportunities and obstacles. This, in turn, enhances our ability to provide innovative and comprehensive solutions to our customers' challenges.
The gross profit percentage for 2021 increased by 70 basis points based on higher product margins, primarily for safety products and overhead/organizational leverage related to higher volumes.

The non-fastener market is larger than the fastener market, we are underpenetrated in the non-fastener market relative to the fastener market, and industrial vending lends itself to sales of non-fastener products.

factors.summary: Geopolitical risks, such as the Russo-Ukrainian War and China's COVID-19 policies, are impacting non-North American markets. However, the company did not explicitly mention these factors as creating material risks or opportunities.

evidence.text: Geopolitical risks, such as the Russo-Ukrainian War and China's COVID-19 policies, are impacting non-North American markets.
The company did not explicitly mention geopolitical risks in the provided text.

factors.summary: Customer diversification is improving, though the company still exhibits some concentration risks, particularly in certain end markets.

evidence.text: It is helpful to appreciate several aspects of our marketplace: First, it is big. We estimate the North American marketplace for industrial supplies is in excess of $140 billion per year (and we have expanded beyond North America) and no company has a significant portion of this market.

Our non-residential construction and reseller business is heavily influenced by manufacturing, oil and gas, and infrastructure spending. In 2021, improving economic business conditions, high prices for commodities such as metals and energy, and tightening facilities utilization produced improving growth rates throughout the year.

factors.summary: Free cash flow performance shows a mixed trend, with an increase in 2022 but a decrease in 2021, primarily due to working capital needs and product inflation during the latter year. The percentage of free cash flow relative to net earnings improved in 2022 but declined in 2021, reflecting varying operational and financial conditions across the years.

evidence.text: "Net cash provided by operating activities in dollars and as a percentage of net earnings were as follows: [...] 2022 2021 Net cash provided $ 941.0 770.1 % of net earnings 86.6% 83.3%"
evidence.subsection: Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities

evidence.text: "In 2021, the decrease in our operating cash flow as a percentage of net earnings was due to significant growth in working capital as we supported growth in our customers' operations as well as, in the case of inventory, significant product inflation."
evidence.subsection: Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities

factors.summary: The company is increasing its headcount, particularly in non-selling roles, to support growth drivers such as Onsite and national account expansion. However, there is a mixed trend with some departments experiencing headcount reductions, particularly in in-market locations, distribution, transportation, manufacturing, and selling roles.

evidence.text: The company increased its headcount across various departments, indicating a growth mode, particularly in areas supporting expansion drivers.

The table below summarizes the percentage change in our FTE headcount at the end of the periods presented compared to the end of the prior period: [...] Total 8.3% 2.8% [...] (1) Our non-in-market selling employee count has grown in recent years due to an increased focus on resources to support our growth drivers, particularly Onsite and national account growth.

The table below summarizes the percentage change in our FTE headcount at the end of the periods presented compared to the end of the prior period: [...] In-market locations (branches & Onsites) 0.7% -8.0% Non-in-market selling (1) 8.0% 5.4% Selling subtotal 1.7% -6.2% Distribution/Transportation 5.8% -10.5% Manufacturing 2.0% -9.9% Organizational support personnel (2) (3) 7.4% 8.7% Non-selling subtotal 5.8% -5.2% Total 2.8% -6.0%

factors.summary: The company's capital expenditure (CapEx) outlook for 2023 is mixed. While there is an expected increase in CapEx compared to 2022, driven by investments in automation, distribution centers, IT, and fleet equipment, there is also a note of decreased spending in 2021 compared to 2020, primarily due to reduced activity in other areas despite higher spending on an office building construction project.

evidence.text: We expect our net capital expenditures in 2023 to be within a range of $210.0 to $230.0. This increase from 2022 reflects: spending on upgrades to and investments in automation at certain hubs; the beginning of construction of a distribution center in Utah; investment in materials to facilitate our branch conversion projects; higher spending on information technology; and investments in fleet equipment to support our network of heavy trucks.
evidence.subsection: Lease Obligations / Net Cash Used in Investing Activities

evidence.text: Our net capital expenditures decreased in 2021, when compared to 2020. We had higher spending on an office building construction project in Winona, Minnesota intended to support growth in our business. This was more than offset by reduced spending in other areas.
evidence.subsection: Net Cash Used in Investing Activities

factors.summary: Shareholder returns have shown a positive trend with increased buybacks and dividend payments in 2022, while 2021 saw consistent dividend payments but no share purchases.

evidence.text: In 2022, we purchased 5,000,000 shares of our common stock at an average price of approximately $47.58 per share. [...] We declared a quarterly dividend of $0.35 per share on January 18, 2023. In 2022, we paid aggregate annual dividends per share of $1.24. In 2021, we paid aggregate annual dividends per share of $1.12.

In 2021, we did not purchase any shares of our common stock. In 2021, we paid aggregate annual dividends per share of $1.12. In 2020, we paid aggregate annual dividends per share of $1.40, which included $1.00 in regular quarterly dividends and a $0.40 special dividend paid in December 2020 as a result of our high cash balances and favorable financial outlook.
